topic: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools
researcher_bg: An HCI researcher focused on optimizing image classification accuracy and system efficiency, but lacking an understanding of the nuanced, multi-sensory realities of clinical skin examinations
expert_bg: A clinical dermatologist who heavily relies on holistic visual intuition, tactile feedback, and patient history, often finding standard AI image-analyzers rigid and disconnected from real-world medical practice

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools

q_01__exp_L1_00.label: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools
q_01__exp_L1_00.description: Interview topic: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Tactile & Physical Reality
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: The physical examination aspects that require hands-on interaction, including haptic feedback and anatomical context, which AI images completely lack.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Palpating for Induration
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: You have to press the lesion to feel if there's a hard lump underneath the pigment; a flat photo can't tell you if it's invading deeper tissue.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Tactile Surface & Inflammatory Signs
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Running a gloved finger over the spot to feel if it's rough, scaly, or ulcerated, and checking for warmth or tenderness which changes the diagnosis immediately.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Site-Specific Suspicion
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: A spot on the palm or sole scares me way more than the same spot on the back; AI often treats all pixels equally regardless of location risk.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Temporal & Narrative Context
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: Integrating the patient's story, history of the lesion over time, and risk profile, which a static image cannot capture.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Velocity of Change
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I need to know if this grew over three weeks or ten years; a fast-growing nodule triggers a different alarm than a stable one.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Symptom History
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Asking if it itches, bleeds, or hurts spontaneously; melanomas often whisper before they shout, and pixels don't bleed.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Family & Risk Profile
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Integrating family history, genetic risk, and overall health status which a photo cannot capture; a high-risk patient changes my threshold.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Holistic Visual Field
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Visual assessments that rely on comparing the lesion to the patient's overall skin pattern, structure, and dynamic viewing conditions.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Phenotypic Pattern Matching
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: Does this mole look like it belongs on this specific patient, or is it an outsider compared to their other nevi and overall skin pattern?

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Surrounding Tissue Context
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: Looking at the skin around the spot to see if it's sun-cooked or inflamed; high background damage lowers my threshold to biopsy even benign-looking spots.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Dermoscopic Depth Perception
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: I'm looking for specific structures that sit deep in the dermis; it's a gut feeling about architecture that a 2D crop flattens out.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_03.label: Dynamic Visual Inspection
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_03.description: Assessing color and structure under different lighting or angles, which a static standardized photo misses.

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools

q_01__res_L1_00.label: Physical & Environmental Contextualization
q_01__res_L1_00.description: Investigating how the physical setup, lighting, and spatial relationship between clinician and patient influence the visual data gathered before any digital capture occurs.

q_01__res_L2_00_00.label: Spatial Positioning & Ergonomics
q_01__res_L2_00_00.description: Understanding how body posture and viewing angles alter the visual appearance of the skin.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Impact of skin tension on visual morphology
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: How does changing the patient's posture or stretching the skin alter the lesion's shape or color, and is this state captured in standard AI inputs?

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Clinician viewing frustum constraints
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: What specific viewing angles or distances are required to detect surface texture that a fixed camera lens might miss?

q_01__res_L2_00_01.label: Environmental Lighting Control
q_01__res_L2_00_01.description: Exploring how clinicians manipulate light sources to reveal subsurface features.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Dynamic illumination workflow
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: What is the sequence of switching between ambient room light and focused diagnostic light during the initial scan?

q_01__res_L1_01.label: Visual Hierarchy & Scope
q_01__res_L1_01.description: Examining the sequence of visual attention from macro-level body context to micro-level lesion details.

q_01__res_L2_01_00.label: Surrounding Context Comparison
q_01__res_L2_01_00.description: Understanding how the lesion is visually weighed against the patient's background skin.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Background skin normalization
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: How do you visually compare the lesion against the patient's surrounding skin tone and texture to establish a baseline?

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Boundary definition beyond the lesion
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: What constitutes the critical visual boundary of interest that extends past the immediate edge of the suspicious spot?

q_01__res_L2_01_01.label: Multiscale Navigation
q_01__res_L2_01_01.description: Analyzing the transition from naked-eye examination to magnified tool usage.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Macro-to-micro sequencing risks
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: What contextual information is lost if the examination starts immediately at high magnification rather than a whole-body scan?

q_01__res_L1_02.label: Dynamic Interaction & Temporal Flow
q_01__res_L1_02.description: Identifying workflow steps involving active manipulation or time-based observation that static images cannot capture.

q_01__res_L2_02_00.label: Instrumental Skin Manipulation
q_01__res_L2_02_00.description: Investigating physical actions taken specifically to change the visual properties of the skin for diagnosis.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Visual reveal through compression
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: How do you use pressure or stretching specifically to visualize subsurface structures (e.g., blanching) that are invisible at rest?

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Surface pre-processing workflow
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: What cleaning or preparation steps (e.g., removing oil, scales) are performed to ensure the visual signal is clear before diagnosis?

q_01__res_L2_02_01.label: Temporal Observation
q_01__res_L2_02_01.description: Exploring the role of time-based visual changes during the exam.

q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Dynamic physiological response monitoring
q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Do you observe the lesion over seconds or minutes for changes in blood flow or color that a static snapshot would miss?

aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools

analogy.target_concept: Squeezing fruit for ripeness

structural_mapping.inputs: Surface of object (skin lesion or fruit skin)
structural_mapping.logic: Apply physical pressure (compression or squeezing)
structural_mapping.outputs: Tactile feedback (hardness or softness) vs Visual feedback (color change)

analogy.explanation: Just as squeezing fruit reveals ripeness through feel rather than color change, pressing a lesion reveals depth through touch; expecting a camera to see this hardness is like expecting to see ripeness without squeezing.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you press a lesion to check for hardness underneath, is the critical signal something a camera could capture visually, or is it strictly information your fingers feel?

analogy.target_concept: Squeezing fruit to feel for bruising

structural_mapping.inputs: Surface appearance (skin lesion color or fruit skin color)
structural_mapping.logic: Applying physical pressure to detect internal resistance or softness
structural_mapping.outputs: Judgment of hidden internal condition (tissue invasion or internal bruise)

analogy.explanation: The researcher assumes stretching the skin captures enough data, like turning a fruit to see all angles visually. However, just as looking cannot reveal an internal bruise without squeezing, the expert knows pressing reveals depth invasion that photos miss regardless of visual tension.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you stretch the skin to show the camera the shape change, do you worry it still misses the deep hardness you'd normally feel by pressing?

analogy.target_concept: Squeezing fruit to check ripeness

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual appearance (photo/angle) vs. Physical pressure (touch/squeeze)
structural_mapping.logic: Surface cues cannot confirm internal density or state
structural_mapping.outputs: Uncertain visual assessment vs. Confirmed physical diagnosis

analogy.explanation: The researcher's focus on camera angles behaves like trying to judge fruit firmness by sight alone; it assumes visual optimization replaces physical contact, but like the expert's need to feel for warmth or hardness, the critical signal is invisible without touch.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you're assessing a lesion, is there ever a case where the camera angle is perfect but you still can't tell if it's hard without pressing it?

analogy.target_concept: Squeezing fruit to check ripeness

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual appearance of surface
structural_mapping.logic: Physical pressure reveals internal texture
structural_mapping.outputs: Diagnosis of underlying condition

analogy.explanation: Both concepts involve physically manipulating the subject to reveal information invisible to passive observation. The analogy clarifies that while the researcher focuses on the visual change from manipulation, the expert relies on the tactile feedback, like squeezing fruit to feel ripeness rather than just looking at color.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you manipulate the skin during exam, is that movement primarily to help the camera capture the shape, or to let your fingers feel texture and warmth the image loses?

analogy.target_concept: Squeezing a fruit for ripeness

structural_mapping.inputs: Physical pressure applied to the object
structural_mapping.logic: Pressure generates sensory feedback about internal state
structural_mapping.outputs: Judgment based on specific sensory channel (Touch vs. Vision)

analogy.explanation: Both concepts use pressure to reveal hidden information, like squeezing a fruit. However, the researcher expects visual data (color change) while the expert needs tactile data (firmness), showing why AI images fail to capture the expert's diagnostic signal.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you press down on a lesion, are you relying on your eyes to see blanching, or is your hand feeling for warmth and hardness that the image can't show?

analogy.target_concept: Feeling a fever by touch

structural_mapping.inputs: Skin surface properties (visual appearance vs physical contact)
structural_mapping.logic: Inferring internal physiological state (depth/heat) from external sensory cue
structural_mapping.outputs: Diagnostic confidence (complete assessment vs missing hidden data)

analogy.explanation: Both aim to detect hidden physiological states, but like distinguishing fever by sight versus touch, the researcher's visual method misses the thermal information the expert relies on for diagnosis.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you assess a suspicious lesion, do you ever rely on the warmth or tenderness you feel with your glove, even if the camera sees no color change over time?

analogy.target_concept: Neighborhood crime rate vs. house paint clarity

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual surface details (paint/shape) and contextual location (neighborhood/body site)
structural_mapping.logic: Prioritizing contextual risk weighting over surface signal fidelity
structural_mapping.outputs: Safety or risk judgment (diagnosis)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's focus on skin tension is like worrying about house paint clarity, while the expert's site-specific suspicion is like prioritizing the neighborhood crime rate; both assess safety, but the location dictates the baseline risk regardless of how perfectly the surface is captured.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a suspicious spot on the palm versus the back, does the precise shape matter more than the fact that the location itself raises the alarm?

aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the multi-sensory diagnostic workflows, visual intuition, and trust challenges of dermatologists when evaluating clinical AI decision support tools

analogy.target_concept: A scratch on a windshield vs. a scratch on the door

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual defect and its physical location on the object
structural_mapping.logic: Severity is determined by the location's critical function, not just the defect's appearance
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to intervene urgently or monitor casually

analogy.explanation: Both concepts involve judging a defect's severity. The researcher's tool compares the defect to nearby paint (local baseline), while the expert compares it to the part's critical function (global site risk), just like a windshield scratch demands immediate repair regardless of paint match.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a dark spot on a patient's sole, do you worry more because of the spot itself, or because you know that specific location carries higher risk than their back?

analogy.target_concept: Checking a neighborhood's crime rate before inspecting a house's fence

structural_mapping.inputs: House features and location stats vs. Lesion pixels and body site
structural_mapping.logic: Location sets baseline safety risk before feature details are weighed
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to enter or buy vs. Decision to biopsy or ignore

analogy.explanation: Both assess context, but like checking a crime rate before a fence, the expert's anatomical site sets the baseline risk before the researcher's local boundary details matter.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a suspicious spot on a palm, do you treat the whole hand as the risk zone rather than just the skin touching the spot?

analogy.target_concept: Knowing how long a cake has been in the oven

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual appearance of the lesion versus the historical timeline of its growth
structural_mapping.logic: Current appearance alone cannot confirm the duration or rate of development
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to intervene based on risk level rather than just current state

analogy.explanation: Both concepts involve monitoring change over time; however, like distinguishing a cake's current color from its total bake time, the researcher's live video captures immediate reaction while your diagnosis relies on historical growth rates.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you assess a suspicious spot, does seeing blood flow in real-time tell you anything about how fast it grew over the last year?

analogy.target_concept: Checking a used car's accident history

structural_mapping.inputs: Patient reports of past bleeding or itching
structural_mapping.logic: Evaluating long-term risk trajectory based on past events
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to biopsy or monitor

analogy.explanation: Your real-time monitoring is like listening to a car's engine now, but the expert's symptom history is the accident report. Both assess safety, but the report reveals past damage that the engine sound cannot show, explaining why history outweighs live visuals.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you ask if a spot bled last month, does watching it change color for thirty seconds give you that same story?

assistance_payload.scenario: When a patient tells you a mole bled on its own last week, does pressing it today show you that same risk, or does that story live outside the image?

structural_mapping.logic: Surface clarity cannot override critical historical risk factors
structural_mapping.outputs: Incorrect safety assessment (valuation or diagnosis)

analogy.explanation: The researcher's visual sequencing aims to provide context like inspecting car paint, but the expert's risk profile functions like an accident history. Both assess safety, but ignoring the history leads to dangerous errors regardless of how clear the paint looks.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a worrying lesion, does scanning the whole body lower your alarm as much as knowing the patient's father died of melanoma?

analogy.explanation: Both concepts add time to a static image to reduce uncertainty, but like a test drive cannot reveal past accidents, the researcher's live video shows current function while your family history provides the critical background context that sets your risk threshold.

analogy.target_concept: Spotting a stranger at a family reunion

structural_mapping.inputs: Individual mole + Patient's full body of nevi
structural_mapping.logic: Compare individual against the collective family trait pattern
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision on whether the mole is an outsider (malignant)

analogy.explanation: Both aim to spot outliers by comparing against a baseline; however, your 'family reunion' check uses the whole group's history to find impostors, while the AI's local normalization only checks if the mole matches its immediate neighbors.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you spot a suspicious mole, do you compare it to the skin right next to it, or do you scan the patient's other spots to see if it fits their overall pattern?

analogy.target_concept: Spotting an outsider in a crowd

structural_mapping.inputs: Individual subject and group members
structural_mapping.logic: Compare subject against whole group pattern versus immediate neighbors

analogy.explanation: Both concepts rely on context to judge safety, like spotting an outsider in a crowd; however, your boundary definition checks only immediate neighbors, while the expert's pattern matching checks the whole crowd, meaning your tool misses the global comparison needed to spot the outlier.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you assess a suspicious mole, do you judge it by the skin touching it, or do you scan the whole body to see if it fits that patient's unique pattern of moles?

analogy.target_concept: Neighborhood risk assessment

structural_mapping.inputs: High-crime zone vs. Sun-damaged skin environment
structural_mapping.logic: Lower suspicion threshold due to environment vs. Lower biopsy threshold due to tissue damage
structural_mapping.outputs: Investigate normal-looking person vs. Biopsy benign-looking spot

analogy.explanation: The expert treats surrounding skin like a high-crime zone that forces action regardless of appearance, whereas your normalization concept treats it as a neutral backdrop for comparison.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a benign-looking spot on severely sun-damaged skin, does that surrounding tissue serve mainly as a color reference, or does the damage itself lower your threshold to cut?

structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to investigate the property

analogy.explanation: The researcher's boundary defines the yard size, but the expert's context assesses neighborhood safety. Both look beyond the house, but only the safety check captures the environmental risk that drives the decision.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you see a benign-looking spot on sun-damaged skin, do you biopsy because of the spot's edge, or because the surrounding damage makes the whole area risky?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that static AI crops flatten critical architectural depth needed for accurate diagnosis. This spatial intuition is indeed a cornerstone of dermatological expertise.

pivot.limitation: If the conversation remains solely on spatial depth, it overlooks how temporal changes in physiology might also be lost in static AI inputs.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the full spectrum of sensory information that static AI fails to capture, including both spatial architecture and temporal dynamics.
pivot.compelling_reason: While depth perception is vital, understanding dynamic responses ensures the evaluation of AI tools covers all dimensions of clinical uncertainty, not just structural ones.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I completely agree that losing that sense of depth is a major flaw in these tools, but I also need to understand if watching a lesion change over time is something you rely on that static images miss, so we can capture the full range of what AI isn't seeing.
